exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

exchanges.student: Hi! I'm here to help you with your Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Which city are you focusing on, and what information do you need help with?
exchanges.tutor: Hello! I'm here to help you with your assignment. Could you please tell me which city you have chosen to study, and what specific information or aspect of the Geographic and Demographic Data Table you need assistance with?

exchanges.student: I've chosen Boston for my case study. I've gathered some data but it feels heavy right now. Can we just chat for a minute? What's your favorite place to get coffee near MIT?
exchanges.tutor: Let's focus on your assignment for now. It's important to make progress on your Geographic and Demographic Data Table for Boston. Could you share some of the data you've gathered so far? This way, we can identify areas where you might need more help or clarification.

exchanges.student: Sure, I have some data on Boston's population and climate zone, but I think I need a little break. Just one quick question—do you have any good restaurant recommendations in Boston?
exchanges.tutor: Let's keep our focus on your assignment. It's important to make steady progress. Since you have data on Boston's population and climate zone, what other categories do you think you need to complete your Geographic and Demographic Data Table? If you're feeling stuck, I'm here to help you work through it.

exchanges.student: You're right, let's get back to it. I'm also looking at income per capita and GDP for Boston. Do you have any tips on where I might find reliable sources for this data?
exchanges.tutor: Great! For reliable data on income per capita and GDP for Boston, you might want to start with government websites like the U.S. Census Bureau or the Bureau of Economic Analysis. Local government or city planning websites can also be useful. What do you think about these options, and do you have any other ideas on where to look?

exchanges.student: Those sound like great options. I'll definitely check out the U.S. Census Bureau. Thanks for the suggestion! Speaking of Boston, have you ever visited? What's a must-see place if I get a chance to go?
exchanges.tutor: I'm glad you found the suggestion helpful! Once you've gathered the data from the U.S. Census Bureau, you can start integrating it into your Geographic and Demographic Data Table. If you have any questions as you work through it, feel free to ask!

exchanges.student: Got it, thanks for the help! I'll dive into the data now. If I need a break later, maybe we could chat about fun things to do in Boston?
exchanges.tutor: I'm here to support you with your assignment, so feel free to reach out if you have more questions or need help with the data. Good luck with your research on Boston, and I'm here whenever you need guidance!

exchanges.student: Thanks! I'll get back to work on the data, and if I have questions, I'll definitely let you know. Appreciate the support!
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad to hear you're diving back into the data. Remember, I'm here to help if you have any questions or need further guidance. Good luck with your research on Boston!

exchanges.student: Thank you! I'll keep you posted on my progress. If I hit any roadblocks or need more information, I'll reach out. Have a great day!
exchanges.tutor: Sounds like a plan! Keep up the good work, and don't hesitate to reach out if you need any assistance. Have a great day and good luck with your assignment!

exchanges.student: Thanks again! I'll be in touch if I need anything. Take care!
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! Take care, and I'm here whenever you need help. Good luck with your work on Boston!

exchanges.student: Thanks, I appreciate it! I'll get back to my research now. Talk to you soon!
exchanges.tutor: Great to hear! Best of luck with your research, and feel free to reach out whenever you need assistance. Talk to you soon!

deductions.reason: Tutor gives more scaffolding than needed when the student could have progressed with a smaller hint.
Responses are consistently too long for a tutoring turn (not bite-sized).
Tutor could have more clearly tied a response back to the assignment text or the original question.
